  <concepts>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.thinking:
The user is observing a session where someone is investigating how to reduce the vibration effect during near-win (聽牌) events in a slot game. They've now found the constants that control the shake effect:

1. BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE = 10 - controls how far the shake moves
2. BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY = 0.03 - controls how fast each shake movement happens

The BigHintShake method uses these constants to create a tween animation that shakes the view up/down/left/right. To reduce the vibration, they would need to either:
- Reduce BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE (less distance = gentler shake)
- Increase BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY (slower = less jarring)
- Or reduce the shakeTime parameter (0.3) that's passed in

This is a discovery observation about how the shake system works and what parameters control it.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Decoded Vibration Control Parameters</title>
  <subtitle>Shake intensity controlled by BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE=10 and BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY=0.03 constants in Game_Define</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE constant set to 10 pixels controls the shake movement amplitude</fact>
    <fact>BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY constant set to 0.03 seconds controls the speed of each shake direction change</fact>
    <fact>BigHintShake method creates four-direction tween (up, down, left, right) using these constants</fact>
    <fact>Shake duration calculated as repeatTimes = shakeTime / (BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY * 5) - 1</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Identified the complete vibration control system in GameView.ts. The BigHintShake method creates a repeating tween animation that moves the game view in four directions (up, down, left, right) using two key constants from Game_Define.ts: BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE (10 pixels) determines shake amplitude, and BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY (0.03 seconds) determines speed. The method receives a shakeTime parameter (currently 0.3 for near-win events) and calculates repetitions. To reduce near-win vibration intensity, there are three adjustment options: decrease BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE for gentler movement, increase BIG_SHAKE_FREQUENCY for slower motion, or reduce the 0.3 shakeTime values passed from SlotReels.ts.</narrative>

<observation>

  <subtitle>m_isHasScatterNearWin flag controls vibration triggers at 36 locations throughout reel logic</subtitle>
  <facts>

    <fact>Near-win activates when scatter count reaches SCATTER_NEARWIN_COLLECT_COUNT and deactivates at SCATTER_NEARWIN_MAX_COUNT</fact>
    <fact>Three vibration triggers fire when m_isHasScatterNearWin is true: lines 3195, 3608, 3904 with BigHintShake(0.3)</fact>
    <fact>Near-win state resets to false at multiple exit points including game resets and reaching max scatter count</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Traced the complete near-win state management flow in SlotReels.ts. The m_isHasScatterNearWin flag is set to true when the scatter symbol count reaches a threshold (SCATTER_NEARWIN_COLLECT_COUNT) during normal gameplay, triggering special behavior including vibration feedback. When active, three specific symbol drop events trigger BigHintShake(0.3) vibrations: regular symbol landing (line 3195), tile matching drops (line 3608), and patch-up symbol drops (line 3904). The flag automatically deactivates when scatter count reaches the maximum threshold (SCATTER_NEARWIN_MAX_COUNT) or during various reset conditions. To reduce near-win vibration, the 0.3 duration parameter at these three call sites needs adjustment.</narrative>

content.thinking: The user added a new constant NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE = 5 to Game_Define.ts. This is creating a separate configuration for near-win shake intensity (5 pixels) which is half of the big shake distance (10 pixels). This is the implementation starting.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>feature</type>
  <title>Added Dedicated Near-Win Shake Constant</title>
  <subtitle>Created NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE constant set to 5 pixels, half the intensity of standard BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE constant added to Game_Define.ts with value 5 pixels</fact>
    <fact>New constant provides independent control over near-win vibration intensity separate from big shake effects</fact>
    <fact>Value of 5 pixels is 50% of BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE (10 pixels), reducing near-win vibration amplitude</fact>
    <fact>Chinese documentation explains this is specifically for near-win symbol drop shake effects</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Added a new configuration constant NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE with value 5 to Game_Define.ts, creating dedicated control for near-win vibration effects. Previously, near-win events used the same BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE (10 pixels) as other major shake effects. The new constant is set to 5 pixels—exactly half the standard shake distance—allowing near-win vibrations to be gentler while preserving the intensity of other shake effects like the big award preview (大獎預告). This separation of concerns enables independent tuning of near-win feedback without affecting other game shake behaviors. The next step requires updating GameView.BigHintShake to accept and use this new distance parameter.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>assets/Script/Game_Define.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>assets/Script/Game_Define.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

content.thinking:
The user is modifying GameView.ts to accept a distance parameter in the BigHintShake method. The signature changed from:
```
BigHintShake( shakeTime: number )
```
to:
```
BigHintShake( shakeTime: number, distance: number = Game_Define.BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE )
```

And the implementation changed from using the hardcoded constant:
```
let dt = Game_Define.BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE;
```
to using the parameter:
```
let dt = distance;
```

This allows callers to optionally pass a custom distance value, while defaulting to the original BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE if not provided. This is necessary to use the new NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE constant that was added earlier.

Now they're reading the SlotReels.ts file to check the call sites where they need to pass the new distance parameter.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>refactor</type>
  <title>Modified BigHintShake to Accept Custom Distance Parameter</title>
  <subtitle>Added optional distance parameter to BigHintShake method, defaulting to BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE for backward compatibility</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>BigHintShake method signature updated to accept optional distance parameter with default value Game_Define.BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE</fact>
    <fact>Shake distance implementation changed from hardcoded Game_Define.BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE to parameterized distance value</fact>
    <fact>Backward compatible design allows existing calls to work unchanged while enabling custom distances</fact>
    <fact>Method now supports per-call shake intensity customization without breaking existing callers</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Refactored the BigHintShake method in GameView.ts to accept an optional distance parameter, replacing the hardcoded BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE constant with a flexible parameter that defaults to the original value. The method signature changed from `BigHintShake(shakeTime: number)` to `BigHintShake(shakeTime: number, distance: number = Game_Define.BIG_SHAKE_DISTANCE)`, and the implementation now uses the parameter `dt = distance` instead of the constant. This backward-compatible design allows all existing call sites (which pass no distance argument) to continue working with the standard 10-pixel shake, while enabling near-win events to explicitly pass the new NEAR_WIN_SHAKE_DISTANCE (5 pixels) for gentler feedback. The next step is updating the three near-win call sites in SlotReels.ts to pass the reduced distance.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>assets/Script/GameView.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>assets/Script/GameView.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>

Map<number, ColumnFallStats>();\\n        const sortedColumns = Array.from(eliminatedRowsByColumn.keys()).sort((a, b) => a - b);\\n\\n        for (const col of sortedColumns) {\\n            const const { col, row } = this.GetColRowFromPosition(pos);\\n            const stats = columnStats.get(col);\\n            if (!stats || row < 0 || row >= stats.visibleCount) {\\n                continue;\\n            }\\n\\n            // 上方仍存在的符號數量 = 需要向上找可用節點的距離\\n            const exitCountAbove = stats.aliveAbove[row];\\n            // 下方被清空的格數 = 需要把補牌多往上提的虛擬距離\\n            const
